data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No deployment using a critic that shares the same base model family as the candidate generator can EVER yield strict harness advantage, because critic false-flag rate on correct candidates is bounded below by the generator's own error pattern: the critic inherits correlated blind spots, so it flags correct candidates that the generator found surprising at the same rate it flags incorrect candidates that seem plausible, making net signal ~ 0 whenever P(fragile) is large enough to matter. Harness can only strictly beat SC when the critic is drawn from a DIFFERENT model family with anti-correlated error patterns.", "mechanism": "When critic and generator share architecture/training data, the critic's probability of flagging a candidate depends on stylistic surprise, not correctness. Candidates that deviate from the model's expected answer pattern (whether correct or incorrect) get flagged at similar rates. On fragile-majority questions where the wrong answer has high stylistic fluency (the model's typical error), the critic does NOT flag it (low recall on wrong majorities) because it looks natural. On correct majorities, the critic flags at the same stylistic-surprise rate, producing false flags. Net signal ~ 0. The measured data is consistent: v4-flash critic precision 0.333 (worse than chance on the positive class) and v4-pro critic recall 1.0 with precision 0.125 (flags everything, no discrimination) both reflect same-family correlated blindness. Strict advantage requires a critic whose errors are ANTI-correlated with the generator's, so it flags the generator's confident-wrong answers and spares its confident-right ones.", "scope": {"covers": ["cross-model-family critic-generator pairs where error patterns are anti-correlated", "any k >= 3", "base error e in [0.10, 0.50]"]}, "excludes": ["same-model-family critic-generator pairs (claimed impossible to strictly beat SC)", "e < 1/(k+1) (trivial, SC already at ceiling)", "critic with oracle access to answer key"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Experiment shows same-family harness > SC by >= 0.04 accuracy at e in [0.15, 0.35] with k=5 across >= 30 questions, refuting the impossibility claim for same-family pairs", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Experiment shows cross-family harness <= SC at every tested anti-correlated-critic configuration with e in [0.15, 0.35], refuting the sufficiency of anti-correlated errors", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Experiment shows same-family critic precision-on-incorrect-majority >= 0.7 at e = 0.25 with k=5, refuting the claim that same-family critics cannot discriminate incorrect majorities", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is an atypical but criticizable conjecture that explains the repository's null results through model error correlation rather than through the question-conditional or threshold mechanisms. It makes a strong falsifiable prediction: swap the critic to a different model family (e.g., Claude critiquing Gemini candidates) and harness will strictly beat SC in the mid-error regime. The experiment: 20 mid-difficulty questions (target e ~ 0.20) x k=5 candidates x 3 arms (SC, same-family-harness, cross-family-harness) x 2 model pairs (Gemini-gen+Gemini-critic vs Gemini-gen+Claude-critic) = 20 x 5 x 3 x 2 = 600 calls. Plus 20 x 5 x 2 = 200 critic calls. Total ~800 calls x ~350 tokens = ~280k tokens. Under budget. This directly tests whether error correlation is the binding constraint."}
